jokes.text: You can sit here, but you can't go to Broadway when I'm selling out show after show for ten weeks. We have an obligation imbalance, okay?
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about relationship dynamics
jokes.punchline: The absurdly formal term 'obligation imbalance' for friendship grievances
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry - turning petty social grievances into bureaucratic language. 'Obligation imbalance' is perfectly pompous and neurotic.

jokes.text: The Prince Charles? Exactly, Mussolini's view.
jokes.setup: Discussion about opera seating positions
jokes.punchline: Comparing side opera seats to dictator's viewing position
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist connection - side opera seats = dictator's balcony view. Economical and unexpected.

jokes.text: No, I said, 'Myoo-solini.' You said, 'Muss-olini.' No, it's not 'Myoo-solini' like 'muesli.'
jokes.setup: Pronunciation argument about Mussolini
jokes.punchline: Comparing dictator's name to breakfast cereal pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Typical Larry pedantry but the muesli comparison adds absurdist flavor. Competent character moment.

jokes.text: I confuse it with Mucilex. I take that for my coughs.
jokes.setup: Trying to explain Mussolini pronunciation confusion
jokes.punchline: Confusing fascist dictator with cough medicine
jokes.explanation: Absurd brain connection - dictator/cough medicine. Good escalation of the pronunciation bit.

jokes.text: What are you... Are we married? I don't... I don't understand. Am I dating you?
jokes.setup: Jeff's complaints about Larry's social behavior
jokes.punchline: Larry's bewildered escalation to romantic relationship terms
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry defense mechanism - turning friend complaints into relationship confusion. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: You're the worst-dressed person I've ever seen. I can't even look at you. I get depressed. When are you gonna die? Will you just... will you please die?
jokes.setup: Fashion insults between friends
jokes.punchline: Escalating from wardrobe criticism to death wishes
jokes.explanation: Classic escalation structure - wardrobe complaints escalating to literal death wishes. Perfectly absurd friendship dynamic.

jokes.text: That's the guy who's in, uh, my new show. Young Larry. He's playing me, when I was in my 20s.
jokes.setup: Larry spotting actor at political event
jokes.punchline: The meta absurdity of Larry pointing out his own fictional younger self
jokes.explanation: Meta-comedy gold - Larry in real life pointing out actor playing fictional Larry. Self-referential but earned.

jokes.text: Believe me, he wants to be tapped.
jokes.setup: Larry insisting stranger should tap actor
jokes.punchline: Larry's delusional confidence about stranger's desires
jokes.explanation: Larry's oblivious social confidence. Good character moment but not exceptional craft.

jokes.text: I do not like being tapped, and I will not tap a stranger.
jokes.setup: Larry's tapping request
jokes.punchline: Irma's firm anti-tapping stance delivered with authority
jokes.explanation: Irma's character establishment - officious and rule-bound. The repetition of 'tap' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You're talking. / No, you're talking. / You're talking, and you keep talking!
jokes.setup: Disruption during political speech
jokes.punchline: The circular 'you're talking' argument while everyone is indeed talking
jokes.explanation: Childish but effective escalation, captures the absurdity of public disruptions

jokes.text: Where'd you get that dress anyway, at a substitute teacher designer warehouse?
jokes.setup: Argument with annoying woman at event
jokes.punchline: Hyper-specific insult combining mundane profession with fashion retail
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Larry insult - 'substitute teacher designer warehouse' is perfectly specific and cutting. Peak observational cruelty.

jokes.text: You mid-level celebrities, you think you're so smart with all your banter.
jokes.setup: Woman fighting back against Larry
jokes.punchline: Dismissing Larry as 'mid-level celebrity' and mocking his 'banter'
jokes.explanation: 'Mid-level celebrities' is a perfect dig at Larry's ego. The word 'banter' adds pretentious dismissiveness.

jokes.text: You mid-level celebrities, you think you're so smart with all your banter.
jokes.setup: Woman responding to Larry's insults
jokes.punchline: Dismissing Larry as 'mid-level celebrity' with pretentious banter
jokes.explanation: 'Mid-level celebrity' is a perfect putdown for Larry, and 'banter' is delightfully pretentious.

jokes.text: You see somebody in a hat that says, 'St. Petersburg, Florida,' do you wanna go there?
jokes.setup: Larry explaining why pins don't work
jokes.punchline: Comparing political pins to unappealing tourist destination hats
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry logic - reducing political persuasion to tourist hat appeal. Brilliant analogy that feels fresh.

jokes.text: Non-pros, there's nothing they love more than when actors tell them what they think.
jokes.setup: Young actor talking about artists' obligations
jokes.punchline: Larry's sarcastic take on celebrity political opinions
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about celebrity activism. 'Non-pros' is a great dismissive term for regular people.

jokes.text: An open bad book, that after ten pages you'll put down, okay?
jokes.setup: Young actor says he's 'an open book'
jokes.punchline: Larry's immediate harsh verdict on the metaphorical book
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry - taking someone's metaphor and immediately destroying it. Perfect timing and cruelty.

jokes.text: Irma Kostroski. She's a city councilwoman, very powerful. The most hideous... disagreeable person.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about the difficult woman
jokes.punchline: Jimmy's diplomatic pause before 'disagreeable person'
jokes.explanation: Good politician's diplomatic restraint - starting to say 'hideous' then pivoting to 'disagreeable.' Classic political speak.

jokes.text: Who lives with his 75-year-old uncle? I do. Why can't you be normal? I'm perfectly normal. Marcia, am I normal?
jokes.setup: Family argument about living arrangements
jokes.punchline: Man defending abnormal living situation by asking for validation
jokes.explanation: Standard cringe comedy - person defending obviously abnormal behavior. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: My mother thinks you're mental! Really? Yes. She told me! Why are you telling me?
jokes.setup: Woman sharing mother's harsh opinion
jokes.punchline: Man's reasonable question about why she'd share that information
jokes.explanation: Good relationship comedy - the logic of 'why are you telling me this?' is relatable and funny.

jokes.text: I've seen the apartment, and it's really nice. And I'm sure I'll be spending a lot of time there. So you can see why... this is a toughie.
jokes.setup: Asked to judge if man is normal for living with uncle
jokes.punchline: Marcia's diplomatic non-answer revealing self-interest
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment - Marcia's self-serving neutrality. 'This is a toughie' is perfectly evasive.

jokes.text: A real mechayeh. He's what? What's that? He's a mechayeh, he's a pleasure. Oh, 'pleasure.'
jokes.setup: Larry using Yiddish term Jeff doesn't know
jokes.punchline: Translation confusion revealing cultural gap
jokes.explanation: Mild cultural comedy - Yiddish confusion. Character-appropriate but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: Do you have any ideas? Yeah. Shoot her.
jokes.setup: Discussing problem with bad actress
jokes.punchline: Jeff's immediate, matter-of-fact violent solution
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery. The economy is flawless - setup and immediate dark punchline.

jokes.text: I feel these mugs, they're not true.
jokes.setup: Young actor complaining about prop mugs
jokes.punchline: Pretentious actor-speak about coffee mug authenticity
jokes.explanation: Perfect pretentious actor dialogue - 'mugs aren't true' captures method actor absurdity perfectly.

jokes.text: It wasn't important at all, Asa! Please, um, would you mind calling me Larry?
jokes.setup: Young actor insisting coffee was important to Larry
jokes.punchline: Larry correcting him while also demanding to be called by character name
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Larry's ego needing to be called by character name while correcting actor's assumptions.

jokes.text: Isn't that what actors do? They fake it?
jokes.setup: Actor saying he can't fake disingenuous things
jokes.punchline: Larry's obvious observation about acting profession
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective logic - pointing out the obvious contradiction in actor's statement. Good economy.

jokes.text: Well, I think Stella Adler would, uh, beg to differ.
jokes.setup: Larry saying actors fake it
jokes.punchline: Young actor invoking pretentious acting teacher reference
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment - pretentious young actor dropping acting teacher names. Standard but effective.

jokes.text: He's the biggest mug here, if you ask me.
jokes.setup: Discussion about actor rejecting prop mugs
jokes.punchline: Larry calling the actor himself a 'mug' (British slang for fool)
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay - using 'mug' to describe the person complaining about mugs. Good callback structure.

jokes.text: I heard something. I don't know if it's true or not, but somebody told me that he was sexually abused... as a young actor when he was, like, 17 or something
jokes.setup: Explaining why they should cut actor slack
jokes.punchline: The 'trauma' being sexual encounter with beautiful actress
jokes.explanation: Sets up the major reveal about the 'abuse.' Dark comedy about what constitutes trauma.

jokes.text: I'll get five more for little prick Larry.
jokes.setup: Stan frustrated with demanding young actor
jokes.punchline: Calling the actor 'little prick Larry' with bitter resignation
jokes.explanation: Good frustrated worker dialogue. The alliteration in 'little prick Larry' adds punch.

jokes.text: And if he rejects those... I can tell you where it'll end up.
jokes.setup: If actor rejects more mugs
jokes.punchline: Implied threat about where mugs will go (up his ass)
jokes.explanation: Good implied threat - letting audience imagine where the mugs will end up. Economical and character-driven.

jokes.text: Unequivocally, the worst acting I've ever seen. Ever!
jokes.setup: Discussing the bad actress
jokes.punchline: Larry's absolute condemnation with 'Ever!' emphasis
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry hyperbole. The 'Ever!' adds emphasis but it's fairly standard character reaction.

jokes.text: Lilly Collins's manager, you know, really... They were really interested in this. She was? Yeah. You shouldn't have even told me that.
jokes.setup: Producer mentioning better actress was interested
jokes.punchline: Larry's immediate regret at hearing what he missed
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Larry's immediate recognition that information just makes him feel worse.

jokes.text: Are you out of your mind? What am I, a Fuller Brush salesman?
jokes.setup: Susie suggesting Larry do canvassing
jokes.punchline: Larry's outrage comparing canvassing to door-to-door sales
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry outrage with specific reference. Fuller Brush salesman is dated but perfect for Larry's sensibility.

jokes.text: They make the law, they can repeal the law. It's just a matter of votes. They gotta get the votes.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about city council power to repeal laws
jokes.punchline: Jeff's obvious explanation that sets up Larry's scheme
jokes.explanation: Important plot setup disguised as explanation. Gains rewatch value when you know Larry's plan.

jokes.text: It's like a dance with death with those scooters for the seniors.
jokes.setup: Discussing senior safety at pier
jokes.punchline: Dramatic description of elderly scooter danger
jokes.explanation: 'Dance with death' is beautifully overwrought for describing senior scooter navigation. Great imagery.

jokes.text: Have you ever thought about maybe running for, uh, senator, or congresswoman? Uh, no? What about a congresswoman? I don't wanna move to D.C. Too cold.
jokes.setup: Larry flattering Irma with political ambition talk
jokes.punchline: Irma's practical rejection based on D.C. weather
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Irma's practical concerns override political ambition. Shows Larry's manipulation starting.

jokes.text: Oh, but don't tell me it's with an 'E.' 'Erm'? With an... No, an 'I.' I have to go.
jokes.setup: Larry complimenting Irma's name
jokes.punchline: Irma's confusion about the spelling conversation
jokes.explanation: Awkward small talk moment - Larry making weird conversation while trying to charm Irma.

jokes.text: Gas here, you know? I get a bubble. Oh, you get a bubble? Yeah. Larry with a 'Y'? Yeah. Yeah, funny.
jokes.setup: Irma mentioning her gas problem
jokes.punchline: Larry's awkward response and her calling his name spelling 'funny'
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - inappropriate personal information mixed with weird small talk. Character-driven awkwardness.

jokes.text: He took the worst aspects of ten different people and put 'em into her.
jokes.setup: Continuing Frankenstein metaphor
jokes.punchline: Specific description of how she was assembled from worst human parts
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the Frankenstein metaphor. The specificity of 'ten different people' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: So you gotta become the... the groom of Frankenstein to get rid of this fucking actress?
jokes.setup: Understanding Larry's romantic scheme
jokes.punchline: Jeff calling Larry the 'groom of Frankenstein'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback and escalation. 'Groom of Frankenstein' is perfect - plays on 'Bride of' but makes Larry the male victim.

jokes.text: How can Frankenstein have a kid? Dr. Frankenstein made love to fucking the bride of Frankenstein, and he had a fucking kid, I guess.
jokes.setup: Extending Frankenstein family metaphor
jokes.punchline: Jeff's crude explanation of Frankenstein reproduction
jokes.explanation: Continues the metaphor but gets a bit convoluted. The profanity doesn't add much to the humor.

jokes.text: How you make a woman like that and not tap it? How? How?
jokes.setup: Frankenstein creation discussion
jokes.punchline: Jeff's crude logic about Dr. Frankenstein's restraint
jokes.explanation: Jeff's character-appropriate crudeness but the joke is getting stretched thin by this point.

jokes.text: Really hate giving up that time slot. Can't a motherfucker live a life? Sometimes, no. Yeah. But that golf, that's white man problems. Yeah. We have golf. You have voting.
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about giving up golf lesson
jokes.punchline: Leon's observation about white versus Black priorities
jokes.explanation: Brilliant social commentary through character. Leon's 'white man problems' versus 'you have voting' is sharp and economical.

jokes.text: Pick a pair of fucking glasses. I'll tell you what. Put these in the maybes. That's it. They're the ones, all right? In the maybe pile.
jokes.setup: Stan frustrated with actor's indecision about glasses
jokes.punchline: Actor still putting decisive glasses in 'maybe' pile
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - even when Stan forces a decision, the actor still won't commit. Shows the absurdity well.

jokes.text: That little Asa there, when he was a 17-year-old kid, was 'taken advantage of' by the beautiful 37-year-old Adrianna Amante, the Italian actress. Fuckin' smoke show! Stunning!
jokes.setup: Revealing the nature of Asa's 'sexual abuse'
jokes.punchline: Stan's enthusiastic description of the 'abuser' as gorgeous
jokes.explanation: Great reveal moment - the contrast between 'taken advantage of' and Stan's obvious envy. Dark comedy gold.

jokes.text: Were you as lucky at 17, to be taken advantage of by a supermodel? Yeah, right. I was traumatized because I didn't have any sex at all.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Asa's 'trauma'
jokes.punchline: Larry's real trauma was the opposite - no sex at all
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - the real trauma was NOT having the experience Asa is complaining about. Brilliant reversal.

jokes.text: If a cactus touched your penis, you would've been thrilled at that age. Okay?
jokes.setup: Larry confronting Asa about his 'trauma'
jokes.punchline: Extreme metaphor about teenage male sexual desperation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist metaphor - cactus penis contact as preferable to no contact. Shocking, economical, and quotable.

jokes.text: They would've named the high school after me.
jokes.setup: Larry asking how he'd be treated for sleeping with famous actress
jokes.punchline: Asa's acknowledgment he'd be celebrated, not traumatized
jokes.explanation: Perfect punchline - acknowledges the obvious truth that he'd be a hero, not a victim. Good self-aware moment.

jokes.text: He's gonna make 'em homed. Homed!
jokes.setup: Canvassing about solving homelessness
jokes.punchline: Irma's enthusiastic made-up word 'homed' as opposite of homeless
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay - 'homed' as the opposite of homeless. Simple but effective linguistic humor.

jokes.text: You can't say 'Happy New Year' after January 7th. I like that. Very smart. Last year I got a Happy New Year during Black History Month.
jokes.setup: Larry making up political positions for Mayhew
jokes.punchline: Voter's enthusiastic agreement and personal example
jokes.explanation: Brilliant fake policy that resonates. 'Happy New Year during Black History Month' is perfectly specific and absurd.

jokes.text: No child under ten allowed in restaurants. Oh, now that's awesome.
jokes.setup: Another fake Mayhew policy
jokes.punchline: Voter's immediate enthusiasm for child restaurant ban
jokes.explanation: Good relatable fake policy - many adults would support this. Shows Larry understanding his audience.

jokes.text: He respects wood.
jokes.setup: Explaining environmental positions
jokes.punchline: Vague, meaningless environmental stance
jokes.explanation: Perfect meaningless political statement. 'He respects wood' is absurdly vague but sounds environmental.

jokes.text: He's eliminating hand shaking. He's my man!
jokes.setup: Final fake policy announcement
jokes.punchline: Voter's ultimate enthusiasm for eliminating handshakes
jokes.explanation: Great fake policy that would actually appeal to many people. Perfect culmination of Larry's made-up platform.

jokes.text: What, you got the Cliff Notes, huh?
jokes.setup: Cheryl and friend reading Faulkner
jokes.punchline: Larry's condescending assumption they're using study guides
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry condescension - can't believe others actually read serious literature. Good character moment.

jokes.text: The Sound and the Fury. / Faulkner. / You two are reading Faulkner? / Yes, we are. What?
jokes.setup: Larry's surprise at Cheryl reading Faulkner
jokes.punchline: Cheryl's defensive reaction to his obvious surprise
jokes.explanation: Sets up Larry's intellectual snobbery vs others' defensiveness

jokes.text: I wouldn't canvass with you because you turn people off, and I didn't... I didn't want you to turn people off.
jokes.setup: Cheryl pointing out Larry's hypocrisy about canvassing
jokes.punchline: Larry's excuse that Cheryl is the one who turns people off
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry deflection - blaming Cheryl for his own antisocial tendencies. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Well, I hate people individually, but I love mankind.
jokes.setup: Cheryl saying Larry doesn't like people
jokes.punchline: Larry's philosophical distinction between individuals and humanity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Larry philosophy - perfectly captures his character. Abstract love for humanity vs. hatred of actual humans. Peak writing.

jokes.text: Well, you know, maybe when I was married to you, you held me back from the person I really wanted to be.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining his newfound political interest
jokes.punchline: Blaming Cheryl for preventing his political awakening
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Larry's ridiculous excuse for his behavior change. Classic blame deflection.

jokes.text: You hate pins 'cause they make holes in your sweater. Well, it's a thin pin. Oh. A thin pin.
jokes.setup: Cheryl catching Larry's contradiction about pins
jokes.punchline: Larry using the same weak excuse from earlier
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback - Larry caught using the same lame excuse. Shows his consistency in weak justifications.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, you have a great interest in turn-of-the-century Mississippi. I do!
jokes.setup: Larry mocking Cheryl's Faulkner reading
jokes.punchline: Cheryl's defiant insistence on her literary interests
jokes.explanation: Good character conflict - Larry's snobbishness versus Cheryl's defensive pride. Well-executed but not exceptional.

jokes.text: You never pee when you canvass.
jokes.setup: Larry asking to use bathroom while canvassing
jokes.punchline: Irma's strict canvassing rule about bathroom use
jokes.explanation: Perfect Irma character moment - rigid rules for everything, including basic human needs. Absurd but believable.

jokes.text: Fucking rat poison! Has to be vegan, of course. You know how hard they are to find in the Valley?
jokes.setup: Complaining about difficult shopping requests
jokes.punchline: The absurd specificity of vegan rat poison
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist complaint - vegan rat poison is perfectly ridiculous. Great specific detail.

jokes.text: He rejected these because he said they were too ''90s.' And he's looking for a '70s vibe.
jokes.setup: Stan explaining Asa's puff rejections
jokes.punchline: Snack foods being rejected for wrong decade vibes
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist complaint - snack foods having decade-specific vibes. Perfect pretentious actor behavior.

jokes.text: Too vegan? What does too fucking vegan mean, Larry?
jokes.setup: Stan listing all of Asa's complaints
jokes.punchline: The logical impossibility of something being 'too vegan'
jokes.explanation: Perfect logical absurdity - you can't be 'too vegan.' Stan's exasperation makes it funnier.

jokes.text: He wants the cups to be dolphin-friendly. What the fuck does that even mean, Larry?
jokes.setup: More Asa complaints
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of dolphin-friendly cups
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist environmental concern - dolphin-friendly cups makes no sense but sounds plausible.

jokes.text: I'm telling you now, man, I'll shove those fingers so far up his arse, they'll pop out of his mouth and he can use them as a hand puppet.
jokes.setup: Stan threatening Asa for finger-flicking
jokes.punchline: Elaborate anatomical threat involving hand puppet
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly elaborate threat - the hand puppet detail makes it absurdly specific and memorable.

jokes.text: Look, they look like something from fucking Starsky and Hutch. Seventies written all over that, Lar.
jokes.setup: Stan's backup snacks for the picky actor
jokes.punchline: Perfect '70s reference to satisfy decade requirements
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the decade requirement. Starsky and Hutch is perfect '70s reference.

jokes.text: This little motherfucker got a piece of ass, which is priceless. Then he got 400,000 dollars on top of that shit? And the movie paid him. He got paid three fucking times, and he still complains.
jokes.setup: Leon's analysis of Asa's 'trauma' situation
jokes.punchline: Leon's mathematical breakdown of Asa's multiple compensation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leon perspective - the practical breakdown of Asa's 'trauma' benefits. Great escalation structure.

jokes.text: How old were you when you first got laid? Fourteen years old. Fourteen? So if you were in the Boy Scouts, you would've got your, uh, intercourse badge.
jokes.setup: Leon sharing his early sexual experience
jokes.punchline: Larry's Boy Scout merit badge comparison
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational humor - 'intercourse badge' is perfectly absurd merit badge concept. Great imagery.

jokes.text: My first time tapping wasn't some gorgeous-ass actress. It was this lady that made donuts and shit, donut shop. You don't see me coming back years later, asking for 400,000 dollars in donuts.
jokes.setup: Leon comparing his first experience to Asa's
jokes.punchline: Leon's practical comparison involving donut shop compensation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Leon logic - comparing actress settlement to theoretical donut compensation. Perfect character voice and absurd escalation.

jokes.text: I tapped the lady that made the holes, you understand?
jokes.setup: Leon's donut shop story
jokes.punchline: Leon's crude wordplay about donut holes
jokes.explanation: Good double entendre - donut holes with sexual implication. Perfect Leon crude humor.

jokes.text: That's Ruth Berman! She's not the widow. She's Berman's ex-wife. They've been divorced for ten years. She's as much a widow as I'm a widow.
jokes.setup: Larry discovering the fake widow
jokes.punchline: Larry's logical comparison about widow status
jokes.explanation: Great Larry logic - the widow comparison is perfectly clear and indignant. Good escalation moment.

jokes.text: You're not a widow, okay? You're the ex-wife of a dead man.
jokes.setup: Larry confronting the fake widow
jokes.punchline: Larry's precise distinction between widow and ex-wife of dead person
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry precision - the technical distinction is both logical and cruel. Great character moment.

jokes.text: No such thing as a thin pin, okay? It doesn't exist.
jokes.setup: Someone using Larry's 'thin pin' excuse
jokes.punchline: Larry rejecting his own earlier excuse
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback - Larry rejecting the same excuse he used earlier. Shows his hypocrisy perfectly.

jokes.text: I'm not here to make friends. I'm here to make art.
jokes.setup: Asa defending his difficult behavior
jokes.punchline: Pretentious artist justification for being an asshole
jokes.explanation: Classic pretentious actor line. Perfect character moment but fairly standard 'artist' comedy.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'd... I'd like that. Yeah, I'd... Down there? Yeah. Fuck yeah. And what if she, uh, touched you down there? And what if she invited you back to her apartment to have sex with her, and then she gave you 400,000 dollars? Who wouldn't take that? Yeah. Who wouldn't take that? Sign me up.
jokes.setup: Larry testing his theory about teenage male sexuality
jokes.punchline: Random guy enthusiastically agreeing to the 'abuse' scenario
jokes.explanation: Good proof of Larry's point about male teenage attitudes. 'Sign me up' is perfect punchline.

jokes.text: If a woman who looks like this was interested in you, what would you say? Yeah, I'd... I'd like that. And what if she, uh, touched you down there? Down there? Yeah. Fuck yeah.
jokes.setup: Larry using stranger to prove his point about Asa
jokes.punchline: Random guy's enthusiastic agreement about the scenario
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the 400k question. The stranger's enthusiasm supports Larry's point effectively.

jokes.text: We kinda cancel each other out. You know, this line? Why don't we get the hell outta here?
jokes.setup: Larry meeting opposing voter in line
jokes.punchline: Larry's logical proposal to skip voting since they cancel out
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry logic - mathematically sound but civically wrong. Sets up the episode's main consequence.

jokes.text: I stand by that statement. And yet for some reason... I'm inexplicably drawn to that which repels me.
jokes.setup: Jeff questioning Larry's pursuit of Irma
jokes.punchline: Larry's philosophical explanation for his contradictory behavior
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-aware Larry philosophy. 'Drawn to that which repels me' is perfect character insight and quotable.

jokes.text: With 100 percent of the precincts reporting, the winner is Jay Reimenschneider. With all precincts in now, it appears as if Mayhew lost by one vote.
jokes.setup: Election results being announced
jokes.punchline: The devastating one-vote margin
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for the climax. The one-vote margin makes Larry's decision catastrophically important.

jokes.text: I know how we lost by one vote. Larry David thought the line was too long, so he didn't vote.
jokes.setup: Everyone wondering about the one-vote loss
jokes.punchline: Irma exposing Larry as the missing vote
jokes.explanation: Perfect climax - Irma's precise recall destroys Larry. The structure is flawless for maximum impact.

jokes.text: I made a deal with a Reimenschneider voter. The line was so long, so we both decided to leave, because we would cancel each other out, so it didn't make a difference.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to defend his voting decision
jokes.punchline: Larry's logical but ultimately flawed excuse
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry defense - mathematically sound but missing the civic responsibility point. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: It was that fucking widow, Ruth Berman!
jokes.setup: Larry being blamed for election loss
jokes.punchline: Larry's final desperate blame deflection to the fake widow
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry finale - blaming the fake widow for the chain of events. Brings all plots together brilliantly.
